name: Lincoln Heights Jail
description: The Lincoln Heights Jail in Los Angeles is a large 1931 Art Deco–era concrete jail complex that closed in 1965 and has sat mostly vacant for years, becoming known for its deteriorating cell blocks, graffiti-covered walls, broken windows, and overall decayed but imposing structure; it has been used occasionally for filming but is not open to the public. The property is fenced off, monitored, and has had private security due to past vandalism and break-ins, and there are reported environmental and structural hazards inside, so entering without permission would be illegal and unsafe.

name: Abandoned Roxie Theater
description: Designed by John M. Cooper, the Roxie was the last theater to open on Broadway in 1931, and the only Art Deco theater in downtown Los Angeles. During its final years it was a Spanish language cinema, and ultimately closed in 1989.

name: Alcatraz Island
description: Alcatraz Island is a small island in San Francisco Bay best known for housing the former federal prison that operated from 1934 to 1963 and once held inmates like Al Capone. Before and after its prison years, it served as a military fort, military prison, and later became a site of Native American activism during the 1969–1971 occupation. Today, it’s part of the National Park Service and preserved as a historic site. You can visit by taking an official ferry operated by Alcatraz City Cruises from Pier 33 in San Francisco; tickets are required in advance, and tours include access to the island grounds plus a self-guided audio tour of the cellhouse.

name: Water reservoir
description: You have to find a gate at the general enterance. You can park at the nearby soccer park. From there find the gate. It is one of those electronic gates only meant to keep out cars so just go over under around or through it. Keep hiking and you will come to a barbed wire fence. Veer to the right and you will find a part of the fence where the barbed wire is cut (likely from a less respectful explorer). From there you can jump that fence easily and head over to the water reservoir. There are three interesting unlocked things. That being the big house like structure with pipes and gears and steam. The second being from an unlocked hatch on the big concrete plane that goes down to some sort of pool of water and odd columns (that is what is pictured in OP’s pictures. Also you can easily climb on top of the reservoir by finding a ladder closed off by a cylindrical locked block for like five feet. Just jump on top of it and then climb the rest of the ladder in order to get to the top of the tower. I couldn’t find a safe way to get inside as the only unlocked opening is at the top of the reservoir and jumping down would be far too hazardous. Great spot for San Diego though. Just enter with caution as there are some security features such as occasional cameras. However, the circuit breaker for the cameras are right beside the cameras themselves so it is not difficult to turn them off.

name: Abandoned Cabin
description: in 1887 Johanson immegrated from Holland and began a lucrative logging company in the Santa Cruz mountains off the road known as Gazos Creek rd. He built three logging cabins in the area around 1900 when logging was in its peak in the area. He sold the land and area in 1930. The area has been logged on and off though the years and this is a remanent of the by-gone era. The area is now Owned by Empire Lumber Co. and is off limits to the general public. The surrounding areas is part of Big Basin and Butano State park.
